By the way, have you used Plexus Earth? You might have even been the one to recommend it to me. But how it works is that people send out their own off-the-cuff thoughts using spoken word, and then the algorithm automatically connects them to related ideas.
woahhh, this is sort of what ISeePattern has been envisioning (https://x.com/ISeePattern)
I think you could use something like this to create a network on half of people, and so essentially act as the "schizophrenic" connector between autistic systems.
when he was pitching this to me

basically I know what the shape of it is, and the question is, what's the easiest way to get from here to there, how to bootstrap this
There is also this other AI knowledge network website that I was using which I have forgotten about. I need to dig it back out of my own personal memory hole.
i think it took me a long time to see this distinction, between the object level research work i'm doing, and infrastructure around it

The idea was just to fractally expand upon knowledge through open-ended exploration of any space of ideas, with the AI there to generate interfaces which give many different possible avenues of exploration. The placeholders are all generated off the cuff, but it actually leads to some pretty good insights.
https://websim.ai/@Csands/core-ai-fractal-knowledge-navigator
Here it is
ok this is also basically it! one reason I've been dragging my feet on this is it feels inevitable/someone else would have done it
but ISeePattern convinced me there is a critical value here of like, finding the people, and connecting them
humans are still pioneering frontiers

I think what might work best is just using experimental interfaces and not building anything that has to be too front-facing, instead just gathering fruits and posting them on main and seeing what sticks, then only from there deciding what systems to fully instantiate. This way the systems follow the insights rather than the other way around.
added that as a resource here: https://github.com/Open-Research-Institute/open-research-institute.github.io/issues/1
yeah i think this is why i was so drawn to the "org chart" ?
i wanted to just make a little profile for reach person, with an entry point to their work
ideally this would automatically be streamed from your twitter profile as you do stuff, and it changes over time
but we're in this transition period now
of going from here to there
I like the idea of creating interfaces for each other. This is basically what quote tweeting allows. But it gets insular when there is no front-facing way of getting new viewers.

Like, if I quote tweet your stuff or if you quote tweet mine, we are basically creating web indexes of each other's work. What follows from there is creating indexes of indexes, and so on. We could think of all research and reference texts as exactly this: organized indexes with a semantic narrative attached.
yes yes yes

there's a chrome extension that IAimForGoat just put out, that scrapes tweets and puts them in the public community archive: https://chromewebstore.google.com/detail/tes-twitter-enhancement-s/abeogphlciedglboobfehkkljdcfgmnb
that can be the missing piece of infrastructure

i built a little viewer for it here, there's still some bugs, but it works in principle (if you scroll through a page of tweets, they get pulled into the archive DB, and our research tools can pull it from there) https://twitter-firehose.glitch.me/

By the way, I recommend that every single user gets their own "desk", where they can spam their own writings and where other people can show up to respond or ask questions. This can be done across platforms, and people can learn where it is best to conduct their desk work and where it is best to find feedback or get engagement. But every platform can have some form of this "desk". In here, that might mean a text channel for the users who want one, with some level of control over moderation.

> "desk", where they can spam their own writings and where other people can show up to respond or ask questions. 

YES
i just love how you already see it
I am in a server for Christian revival where I am the acting president, and I spam a bunch of writings on my desk there. I might try to connect my desks in the future.
i've been painstakingly describing this for a long time and people don't see it

But it is also nice to have different desks in different contexts. I already treat Twitter as my entire desk, and my timeline is my inbox of queries, most of which I ignore but many of which I "cite" in my own writings.

like a discord bot that just streams stuff from that specific channel, to either your ORI profile, or like a "stream" within your profile?

I definitely think it would be better to decentralize any monetary or compensational system at the beginning, since the institution of such a system would instantly shift incentives in a way that might be misaligned.
does it make sense to have like, one Defender profile, or like "Defender (memetics)" and "Defender (theology)", idk yet

You can imagine that some of my different communities would not appreciate all of my thoughts that I have within the context of other domains, so liberally using copy-paste is actually a good way of preventing the "reply-all" situation that happens in orgs. Though, I could definitely connect related desks.

the biggest challenge is encoding the content as a chain of sentences so that we can find overlaps with high-accuracy embeddings, bc document embeddings are extremely lossy
our first assignment should be prototyping a solution for that
we should create a benchmark of content that we know has a lot of overlaps, and then try to encode it in a way that makes it possible to detect and lay out those overlaps

An important function of individual "desks" or "logs" is being a place from which we can forward documents, and also a place into which we can forward documents. This kind of movement of excerpts is exactly why hyperlinked systems like Obsidian and Twitter are so good for insight building and mind mapping.

There is also the important role of public notes that let you see people's writings as they wrote them and learning through their voice. But a more voiceless and distilled resource would allow for faster transmission of ideas. It would have to be provably safe, though.

also <@301295717241323522> one inspiring thing is recognition that ORI is valuable not because of a specific product or web app but because of the community, and how it functions. (like im considering writing a substack post about what this is and inviting people to like, comment on it so I can just keep updating the article with "so & so is on hte frontier of this, is currently working on this. If you are also working on this, contact him on twitter")
like manually doing the curation/directory until we build the thing

Manual attempts at doing the thing that is meant to be automated is basically the same as creating proof of concepts by hand, or doing technical mockups. It is good to dream, even if it uses a fiction to do so. After all, speculative writing is but the academia of the future.

ORI also could be the acronym for "Outsider Research Institute", which actually describes the mission of this project fairly well. It is not just about an open source project of connecting human knowledge, but about having a public-facing infrastructure to develop on ideas *not necessarily* sanctioned within the academy. I have no idea about others participating in this project, but I have no notable academic credentials, and mostly do my own writing and thinking as an online mercenary.

This project, if described purely in functional terms, would be something like a multiplatform plaintext extractor repository. It "sucks up" participating corpuses, and then uses it to generate useful networks which encourage more participation. The expansion loop depends on utility being generated through using this repository for useful connections, which in turn is *only* possible through participation in the activity of networking.

Hi tim - I've done something similar before where I took scattered information about companies and extracted JSON and did the same with funding opportunities. In that way I could compare apples to apples if you know what I mean

how I decided to make it simple for companies to enter information about themselves was by filling in a profile that isn't very rigid design wise - and I would just summarize all that into the json I would need to make the comparison

like lets say one wanted to create a bot that creates job applications - one would just need to fill in a very broad "profile" once - and update that over time - and that service would create many different "CV's" by comparing the original profile with the work profile

This sounds like what I am talking about, yeah. I suppose the comparison in this case is some kind of system-agnostic catalogue of different structures of knowledge which then act as analogies to plug and play in different contexts. It basically does the "interdisciplinary" work without actually explicitly unveiling the specific discipline, or at least the specific disciplinarian (who sets the pertinent species straight).
The more personal and specific corpus feeds into a symbolic model which is then used *by analogy* to make connections elsewhere.

This also reminds me of a thought I had regarding the use of AI to create text-based persistent open world games. Even though AI can freely improvise on people's prompts, this often cannot create consistent worlds under more rigorous interpretation. And so, if there were to be such an open-world game, it would need to have a backend which maintains a log of pertinent structural information about the world, which then serves as an evolving (forward-in-time) canon from which future outputs are generated with the help of user input.
this is good, I think we could make this quite simple UI wise
also, nice to meet you
structuring the user input is important because it lets us set the basics for matching -
Great to meet you, too. Thanks for being here!

I think that it is useful to both maintain the exact composition of the textual corpus, almost like a house of representatives (where the representatives are the raw text), and also to create a group of models, equally represented between inducted members, which each form or simulate one cohesive systemic world and act as members of a senate. This bicameral approach would allow for both the specific personality of the text to shine through, and also allow for the documentation and development of *structures* over merely symbolism.

Taking inputs both from raw population and from joint sovereignties is essentially the reason why the separation between "lords / senators" and "commons" is so common across *political* systems (which themselves represent national "minds"). The House of Representatives is the schizo right brain, concerned with the development of a joint orality; the Senate is the autistic left brain, concerned with the development of federal literacy.

I think what we're really getting at is that deciding which fields we extract is the cornerstone for matching. It's about identifying the essence of the input (to use your terminology) and understanding both its strengths and boundaries.
We'd be mapping where a person's knowledge excels and where their current worldview might be limiting. This extraction process defines the direction for all subsequent matching - it's what makes the "apples to apples" comparison possible in the first place.
The structure we choose to extract determines what patterns we can recognize across different domains. It's like creating a universal translation layer that allows knowledge from one area to inform another, even when the source contexts appear completely unrelated on the surface.

The practical challenge is finding a model that's both cost-effective and scalable while still capable of extracting the nuances in someone's writing. I've mainly worked with the more expensive models for this kind of semantic extraction, but I've heard that open source alternatives like DeepSeek might hit the sweet spot

One question which I am not sure how to answer due to my limited technical expertise is: would the act of predicting a symbolic corpus be enough to get around the barriers inherent to interpreting a corpus and actually seeing "patterns" in it? I suppose so, since in the end the structure itself is symbolic and many analogies will simply emerge in the corpus as they do within human thought. Even if *no* novel symbols are generated, it might generate novel significance as read by the reader, which if documented via more symbols does the exact same thing as generating new significance.

(It also helps us use AI in a way that puts the user at the center - helping them extract the nuance of what they're saying to create matches with people who might otherwise be alienated or deemed "not academic enough." These are sometimes the very people who need to find guidance the most.
This approach democratizes knowledge-sharing by focusing on the underlying structures and patterns of thought rather than academic credentials or specialized vocabulary. We can bridge divides between formal and informal knowledge bases, connecting people who might have complementary insights but speak entirely different languages about their domains.
By extracting the essence of someone's understanding - regardless of how they express it - we create pathways for knowledge to flow where it's most needed, not just where it's most credentialed.)

Also might help to have a way of retraining new models on the same corpus and on all former models as they are (maybe via interviews and some sort of interrogative process?), and then adding those new models to a continuous body of authorities. This way, there is the continuation of "tradition" within the network, while also allowing the development of new voices. A retirement process would have to exist too, though.

One of the beautiful things about ChatGPT is that it purely cares about symbols but in doing so becomes a great way of steel manning viewpoints. Rather than doing the human thing of going right to assuming motives, text generation models answer purely on the object level and in a predictive "helpful" fashion, in essence doing the work of offering charity to every worldview. It needs to create a *model* of a worldview rather than only answer it within its *own* model, and so is the equivalent of a person who comes into a conversation *expecting* to learn.

OpenAI has this feature called Structured Outputs (evolved from their JSON mode) that could be perfect for what we're discussing. It ensures the model outputs follow a specific JSON structure we define.
The beauty of this approach is that we can extract consistent structured data from any input text. So when someone contributes their writing or thoughts, we define the key attributes we want to extract - the "essence" as you put it - and the model consistently pulls those elements out in a standardized format.
This creates compression on both ends, just like you said. Instead of comparing one large body of work with another, we're comparing their distilled essence through a consistent schema.

I suppose one problem is that this might ossify a model of a corpus instead of seeking to just model it in a more freeform fashion. So if a structure is actually outlined explicitly, it would have to be updated *structurally* over time, as well.

I also suppose that maybe these individual "indexes" can just be redone on a regular (or even ad hoc) basis, and so always start fresh, always trying to best model and predict new textual species. Past models can be used as research data.
An analogy is exactly a dynamic index, which sees to match the structure, the isomorphism, via displacement.

Just like how philosophical systems can be seen of as analogies to each other and roughly fit into some buckets, models of corpuses (from which descriptive philosophies emerge) can also be matched, which might lead to many interdisciplinary surprises. It might also lead to non-interdisciplinary surprises, like learning that another system ostensibly under the same label actually fits closer with another ideology *structurally* than it does with yours despite a closer identification.

I am a non-technical systems thinker. You could consider me an experimental theologian, specifically within orthodox classical theistic Nicene Christianity. I derived many systems insights from stints in contest math, piano performance, and music pedagogy.

In terms of what I contribute, I honestly think that the best I can do is write within the corpus, but while doing so also talk about the direction of the project. This way, the project will have writings about itself within it, and so will necessarily develop a cybernetic focus. This was one of the insights that Luhmann had about his card system, which commented on the card system itself.
Ah, that's nice!

I'm a product guy I like to make products easy to use and work with developers to keep the user experience in mind. I am also looking forward to contributing with my own writings that I think can help people who are stuck in complexity or even losing a grip of reality.

one of the things that's super 🔥 to me is I feel like I have identified enough pieces of scaffolding here to link between the "anonymous internet researcher" and the old guard, like in a productive way. light knowlege

you don't recite

this is when I stopped feeling scared that it would disappear
that all these insights would disappear
they weren't going anywhere, i was generating them, and I could generate an _infinite_ amount of them, because I had _the model_
like sampling points on a mathematical curve
you keep collecting points and they're kind of noisy and there's so much detail
but at some point you realize you can just toss all the points

added your take 

One weighs you down; the other sets you free.

This light knowledge is the best kind of knowledge. It's fluid, not fixed. Each time you access it, you naturally express it in novel ways tailored to the situation. If you grow and come back to the knowlege it will have grown because you have grown. It's not memorized or recited and it grows with you—you're not anxious about forgetting it because it's become part of you.

Think about how you explain swimming to different people: to a fearful child, a competitive athlete, or an elderly person trying it for the first time. You don't regurgitate the same explanations from your mental manual—you spontaneously generate the perfect explanation for that person. That's light knowledge at work. It's the ultimate compression algorithm for wisdom.

It's what Einstein hinted at when he said that if you truly understand something, you can explain it to a five-year-old. Not because you've memorized a simplified version, but because you comprehend it so deeply that you can generate any version needed on the fly.

updated section: 

Vipassana, meaning "to see things as they really are," is one of India's most ancient meditation techniques. It's the original meditation method taught by the Buddha, entirely free from dogma. Preserved meticulously through an unbroken chain of over 50 teachers in Burma, Vipassana remained pure and undiluted—even as it evolved into Buddhism elsewhere. Today, this pristine form of meditation is making its way back into the mainstream and can be learned by anyone in just 10 days.

It also is a great way to help people situate ideas within the knowledge network *before* they start writing a piece. It is something like looking to the history of biblical interpretation before you preach a sermon on a scriptural passage. If you start from total scratch, you run the risk of either reinventing the wheel or starting the conversation far beyond where lay people have an easy entry. This is why Twitter, being inherently in itself a mind map of discourse, is such an effective format for a certain category of thinkers.

What are the most novel parts of my research? Probably something to do with how systems insights can apply to defining morality (sin), organizing religion, developing theology, and forming secular ethics influenced by Christianity. None of these things are really that new, but I am approaching them from the angle of presenting one coherent ontology which lets Christians do the rationalist thing of overspecifying for the sake of elaboration within a system without needing to define terms every single time.
excited to continue synnergizing <@1221182134950301706> 🙂

Every single community has its own rituals, but every small fragment of the community also has its own smaller rituals. I expect that more spaces of this sort have *already* been founded, and *will* be founded in the future. What will be the general shape of the rituals that we will expect to take place? How are we to align to them, anticipating how a particular form of community will slowly develop over time given a particular kind of focus?

Looking forward and back to the types of communities that were created like the one that Defender intends will give us more insight into how to proceed with the beginning stages of organizing. One of the easy parallels is, of course, the republic of letters that existed during the Enlightenment. https://x.com/sunriseoath/status/1895538296773886407

Discord is much harder to link to. It would be nice if there were a central online repository to link to, one that is made specifically for the purpose of easy cross referencing.
